title: Rails Secret Key Base secrets should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Rails Secret Key Base detected. Rails secret_key_base is a critical security credential used to sign and encrypt session cookies, verify CSRF tokens, and protect encrypted cookies in Ruby on Rails applications. This long hexadecimal string (typically 64-128 characters) is the foundation of your Rails application's session security. Anyone with access to this secret key can forge session cookies to impersonate any user including administrators, bypass authentication completely, modify encrypted cookie data, forge CSRF tokens to execute unauthorized actions, decrypt sensitive data stored in encrypted cookies (credit card details, personal information), hijack user sessions to access accounts and perform actions as those users, create backdoor admin accounts, and completely compromise your application's security model. Exposing this key means attackers can gain unauthorized access to any user account without credentials, access admin panels and privileged functionality, modify application state through forged requests, steal sensitive cookie data, create persistent unauthorized access, manipulate payment and transaction data, and fundamentally break all session-based security in your application. Immediately generate a new secret_key_base by running 'rake secret' to create a 128-character hex string, update Rails.application.credentials or environment variables with the new key, restart all application servers and background workers to load the new key, invalidate all existing user sessions (users will need to log in again), rotate all related encryption keys and secrets, audit application logs for suspicious session activity during the exposure period, review all session-based operations for unauthorized access, monitor for unusual authentication patterns or privilege escalations, update deployment systems (Heroku, Docker, Kubernetes) with the new SECRET_KEY_BASE, implement session token rotation and monitoring, review all encrypted cookie usage for potential data breach, consider implementing additional session verification (IP checking, user agent validation, anomaly detection), and document this security incident with timeline and impact assessment. The secret_key_base is stored in config/secrets.yml, config/credentials.yml.enc, or as the SECRET_KEY_BASE environment variable, and must be kept absolutely confidential as it is the master key for all session security in your Rails application.

Rails Secret Key Base detected in variable assignment. Rails applications use secret_key_base to secure session cookies, encrypted cookies, and CSRF protection - it's the master key for all session-based security in your application. This critical secret enables Rails to cryptographically sign session cookies (preventing tampering), encrypt sensitive cookie data, generate and verify CSRF authenticity tokens for form submissions, secure ActionDispatch encrypted cookies, and maintain the integrity of all session-based authentication. When this key is compromised, attackers can forge valid session cookies to impersonate any user (including administrators) without knowing their passwords, completely bypass login authentication by creating valid admin sessions, modify encrypted cookie contents to escalate privileges or access restricted data, forge CSRF tokens to perform unauthorized state-changing operations on behalf of legitimate users, decrypt previously encrypted cookies to extract sensitive information like credit cards or personal data, create persistent backdoors through forged authentication cookies, hijack active user sessions mid-transaction, manipulate cookie-based authorization checks, and fundamentally break the trust model of cookie-based session management. The impact extends beyond simple unauthorized access - attackers can access payment processing functions with forged admin sessions, modify transaction amounts through cookie manipulation, steal user credentials stored in encrypted cookies, create fake audit trails by forging authenticated requests, pivot to other systems using session-based single sign-on, execute financial fraud through forged user sessions, access and exfiltrate customer databases, manipulate business logic through session state tampering, and maintain long-term unauthorized access even after password changes. Immediately take the following recovery actions: generate a completely new secret_key_base using 'bin/rails secret' or 'rake secret' (produces a 128-character hex string), update your secret_key_base in config/credentials.yml.enc using 'EDITOR=nano bin/rails credentials:edit' or set SECRET_KEY_BASE environment variable in your deployment platform, deploy the new key to all servers, containers, and worker processes, force invalidation of all existing sessions by clearing the session store (Redis, database, or memcached), restart all Rails application processes to load the new secret, require all users to log in again with their credentials, rotate any other secrets that may have been exposed alongside secret_key_base, audit application and web server logs for unauthorized session activity, check for suspicious admin account creation or privilege escalations, review database access logs during the exposure window, monitor authentication patterns for anomalies, update all deployment configurations (Heroku config vars, Kubernetes secrets, Docker environment files, CI/CD pipelines), implement additional session security (store IP addresses, user agents, implement session timeout), consider implementing session versioning or additional integrity checks, document the incident with timeline and scope of potential impact, notify security team and stakeholders about the breach, and review code repositories and deployment logs to identify how the key was exposed to prevent future incidents.
